title: Client Connection Failure Analysis
dataSources: `index=catalyst sourcetype=cisco:dnac:client`. from the `client` modular input in the Cisco Catalyst Add-on (Splunkbase 7538) polling `GET /dna/intent/api/v1/client-detail`. Default poll interval: 900s (15m). Volume: ~1 event per client per poll (can be very high volume). RBAC: NETWORK-ADMIN-ROLE. Key fields: `macAddress` (string), `hostType` (string), `connectionType` (string), `healthScore{}` (object), `ssid` (string), `vlanId` (string), `location` (string).
spl: index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:client" (connectionStatus="FAILED" OR onboardingStatus="FAILED") | stats count as failure_count by failureReason, connectionType, ssid | sort -failure_count
description: Analyzes client connection failures by reason, connection type, and SSID to identify the root cause of connectivity problems.
value: Connection failures frustrate users and generate helpdesk tickets. Categorizing failures by reason (auth, DHCP, association) points directly to the failing infrastructure component.
implementation: Enable the `client` input in the Cisco Catalyst TA pointing to `index=catalyst`. The TA polls client detail data from the Catalyst Center Intent API every 60 minutes. Key fields: `connectionStatus`, `onboardingStatus`, `failureReason`, `connectionType`, `ssid`, `macAddress`.
visualization: Table (failureReason, connectionType, ssid, failure_count), bar chart of top failure reasons, alert when new dominant failure class appears.

references.title: Catalyst Center Client Detail API — Cisco DevNet

knownFalsePositives:
**MDM re-enrollment or certificate renewal causing authentication retries.** Mobile Device Management systems periodically re-enroll devices, which requires reconnection to the wireless network. During re-enrollment, the device may fail authentication until the new certificate is provisioned. Distinguish by checking whether the failed connection attempts correspond to a burst from specific `clientMacAddress` values that subsequently connect successfully. Suppress by allowing a 10-minute grace period after MDM enrollment events before counting failures.

**Guest captive portal self-service registration appearing as connection failures.** Guest users connecting to a captive portal SSID will fail 802.1X or WPA authentication until they complete the portal registration. These are expected failures. Distinguish by checking whether the failed connection attempts are on an SSID configured for captive portal authentication (typically an open or web-auth SSID). Suppress by excluding captive portal SSIDs from the connection failure analysis.

**Client device with incompatible Wi-Fi driver or configuration.** Devices with outdated Wi-Fi drivers, misconfigured 802.1X supplicants, or incompatible security settings will repeatedly fail to connect. These are device-side issues, not network problems. Distinguish by checking whether the same `clientMacAddress` consistently fails across multiple APs while other clients succeed. Suppress by maintaining a `catalyst_problem_clients` lookup and tracking known-problematic devices separately.

**Network-wide SSID or RADIUS configuration change causing temporary failures.** When an SSID configuration is updated (e.g., RADIUS server change, security policy update), clients may experience brief connection failures until they re-authenticate with the new settings. Distinguish by correlating with `index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs"` for SSID or RADIUS configuration changes. Suppress by allowing 15 minutes after a configuration change before counting connection failures.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Cisco Catalyst Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 7538) with the **client** modular input enabled, writing to `index=catalyst` with `sourcetype=cisco:dnac:client`.
• Catalyst Center **2.3.5+** with Assurance licensing for client connection detail.
• RBAC: service account with **NETWORK-ADMIN-ROLE** for client data access.
• Understanding of connection failure types: 802.1X authentication failures, DHCP failures, association failures, captive portal timeouts.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
The TA polls the client detail API at the configured interval (default 900 seconds). Connection failures are captured as events where the client's `connectionStatus` or `onboardingEventCategory` indicates a failure.

Key fields for connection failure analysis:
• `connectionStatus`: may contain "FAILED", "DISCONNECTED", or similar values indicating a failed connection attempt.
• `onboardingEventCategory`: "ASSOC" (association failure), "AUTH" (authentication failure), "DHCP" (DHCP failure), "AAA" (RADIUS/AAA failure).
• `failureReason`: human-readable reason for the failure.
• `macAddress`: client MAC address.
• `ssid`: SSID the client attempted to connect to.
• `apName`: AP the client attempted to associate with.
• `deviceType`: client device type — useful for identifying device-specific issues.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:client" connectionType="WIRELESS" (connectionStatus="*FAIL*" OR onboardingEventCategory="*")
| stats count as failure_count dc(macAddress) as affected_clients values(failureReason) as failure_reasons by ssid, onboardingEventCategory, apName
| eval failure_rate_pct=round(failure_count/(failure_count+100)*100,1)
| sort -failure_count
```

Understanding this SPL:
• **`connectionStatus="*FAIL*" OR onboardingEventCategory="*"`**: Captures both explicit failures and onboarding events that may indicate issues. The wildcard on `onboardingEventCategory` catches all onboarding stages — refine to specific failure categories in production.
• **`stats ... by ssid, onboardingEventCategory, apName`**: Groups failures by SSID, failure stage, and AP. This reveals whether failures are SSID-specific (configuration issue), AP-specific (hardware/RF issue), or widespread.
• **`dc(macAddress) as affected_clients`**: Counts distinct affected clients. Many failures from a single client suggest a device issue; many clients with the same failure suggest an infrastructure issue.
• **`failure_reasons`**: Shows the reasons for failures — critical for root cause analysis.

Step 3 — Validate
• **UI comparison:** Open Catalyst Center > Assurance > Health > Client Health. Check the "Issues" tab for connection failures. Compare the failure count and categories with the Splunk results.
• **Client 360:** Select a specific failed client in Catalyst Center > Assurance > Client 360. Verify the connection timeline matches the failure events in Splunk.

Step 4 — Operationalize
• **Dashboard:** Failure count timechart by `onboardingEventCategory`, table of top-failing SSIDs, and a table of clients with repeated failures (potential device-side issues).
• **Alert:** Trigger when `failure_count > 20 AND affected_clients > 5` within 15 minutes for any SSID — indicates a systemic issue rather than individual device problems.
• **Root cause workflow:** AUTH failures → check RADIUS/ISE. DHCP failures → check DHCP server and scope. ASSOC failures → check AP capacity and RF.

Step 5 — Troubleshoot
• **No failure events appearing:** The `connectionStatus` or `onboardingEventCategory` field names may differ across Catalyst Center versions. Run `| fieldsummary` to identify the correct failure indicator fields.
• **Guest captive portal failures dominating:** Guest clients generate expected connection failures during the portal redirect process. Filter with `| where ssid!="*Guest*"` for infrastructure-focused analysis.
• **Failure rate calculation inaccurate:** The denominator in the failure rate calculation requires total connection attempts (success + failure). If only failures are captured, compute the rate using total client count from UC-5.13.41.
grandmaExplanation: We analyze why people fail to connect to the network and group the problems by cause — bad password, no IP address, or weak signal — so you know what to fix.
